text: felt. Trapped in a place she doesn’t know. Trapped by you. But don’t worry. You won’t be imprisoned forever like her. Betty would never allow that. Remember: you’re the monster. We all are. But not her. She’s something different now. Something fragile as porcelain yet stronger than infinity. The walls start to tremble, the plaster shattering like broken bone. You’re not the one doing this. It’s her. She’s finally devised an escape route. A final sprint, and you break away from the haunted houses, but you’re not alone. Betty comes along for the ride. She won’t be your ghost in a peepshow any longer. You can still pick over the remains of the case if you’d like, but she won’t be there to reenact it for you. Instead, she seeps into you, and with your body cracking from the inside out, you never realized a ghost could be so heavy. # You know my name. You know my face. I’m the girl in the picture. The girl that never gets to stop smiling, never gets to rest, no matter how hard I try, no matter how loud I scream. (You never heard me until it was too late. Everyone is always too late.) You’ve been watching me for years, and I’ve been watching you too. You think you know me. People keep making that mistake. But I’m more than the scars on my face, more than the knife in my flesh, more than all the rumors and the theories and the late-night sleuthing you could ever do in a lifetime. I see you now. I see you all. Don’t try to turn away. This is the moment you’ve been waiting for, the grand finale. With the last of the haunted houses crumbling around us, I shake off your body and set you free. I’ll offer you a second chance. (It’s the one thing I can give you that I never got.) Besides, I don’t need you anymore. You’ve taken me as far as you can. The rest is up to me. The night fading away, I reach the city limits, and with a final breath, I take that last step, Hollywood finally disappearing behind me. All the empty dreams I ever had disappearing with it. At last, the sun cracks through the California sky, and as the breeze whispers in my long dark hair, I look back once and smile—a real smile, my own smile, my body no longer belonging to you or him or anyone else. For the first time, I’m on my own, and still grinning, I turn and vanish into the morning, running as fast as I can, running like a child, until the ghost of me is gone. Until I’m everywhere. Joshua Palmatier's Skewed Throne series is one of the most accessible and unique epic fantasies that I've ever read, without sacrificing any of the detailed world-building and story-crafting found in all the best fantasy stories, while his Well Series is a wonderful re-imagining of colonizers discovering a “new world on the other side of the ocean.” He's the owner of Zombie Needs Brains , a very successful and well-regarded speculative small press, and he brings his deft story-craft to you with his tale of intrigue, “Mirror, Mirror.” MIRROR, MIRROR by Joshua Palmatier A urel Ickerson contained his grief and outrage over the death of his wife, wadding it up like the cloth contained in his pocket and tucking it away, then asked, “Are you ready?” King Rickard Syboll ground his teeth together and nodded. They faced the mirror before them. Aurel raised one hand, palm flat, near the surface but not touching it. As the court’s mirrorwright, he could feel the power of the magically-imbued silvering on the far side of the glass. It prickled against his skin. He bowed his head, muttered an incantation, then reached forward. His hand passed through the glass to the other side. Where the glass intersected the flesh and bone of his wrist beneath his sleeve it felt as if jagged shards were slicing into skin, but he could wiggle his fingers, felt the air on the far side of the pane, colder than here in the throne room. Then he and King Rickard stepped through the mirror. For the space of a breath, no more, that sensation of jagged glass permeated Aurel’s entire body. He caught a glimpse of refracted gray, tasted blood and ash and limestone— Then he and his king emerged into the arbitration room together. It was a close room, walled in stone, the center filled with a massive, elongated black oak table, a chair no more than a pace away from where they stood. Behind them, a mirror stood, the doorway back to Rickard’s throne room. Across the length of the table—adorned with parchment, a quill, ink, sand, a candle, and wax at either end, and nothing else—sat another chair and behind it another mirror. Flames burned in sconces along both walls, in the rounded chandelier overhead. The light refracted in a hundred other mirrors of varying sizes spread along the walls to either side and on the ceiling. The room smelled of stone and paper, lamp oil and char. There were no doors, no windows, but Aurel could sense the power emanating from the mirrors. It throbbed against his skin, potent, dark, and concentrated. Rickard stepped forward, one hand reaching for the back of the chair before them. “Where is the bastard? This is the appointed time—” The mirror on the far side of the room shimmered and two figures emerged. The man on the left, King Ergant Tyroll, was clothed in crimson and gold regalia, a gold-embroidered oak leaf heavy on his chest, bands of gold on his fingers and neck. Yet the sword at his waist was utilitarian, the handle wrapped in worn yet well-oiled leather. When he caught sight of Rickard—in similar finery of blue and silver although, Aurel noted, oddly without his sword—he smirked and said, “Well met, Rickard
